compile.output: Compilation successful with minor format truncation warnings in date conversion function (non-critical)

validation.comparison_result: 3 out of 10 rows differ. Expected revenues: 440K, 439K, 432K, 428K, 426K, 425K, 425K, 423K, 421K, 415K. Actual revenues: 229K, 218K, 213K, 210K, 209K, 206K, 204K, 200K, 200K, 197K. Both order keys and revenue values are completely different.

Q3.notes: Query produces incorrect results - revenues are roughly 50% lower than expected, indicating partial aggregation failure

Q3.bottleneck: Correctness issue: Incorrect aggregation merge in parallel execution
Q3.root_cause: The parallel aggregation implementation (lines 293-304) does NOT properly merge partial aggregation maps from different threads before Top-K selection. Each thread maintains a local hash map (agg_maps[thread_id]) with partial sums for each (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority) key. However, when building the Top-K heap, the code iterates through all thread-local maps and treats each entry as independent, without first summing revenues for the same key across threads. Result: if orderkey X has lineitems processed by multiple threads, only the partial sum from one thread is considered for Top-K, leading to significantly underestimated revenues (~50% of correct values).

analysis.overall: Q3 produces incorrect results due to missing aggregation merge step in parallel execution. The query compiles and runs in 930ms, but revenues are roughly half of expected values because partial aggregations from different threads are not properly combined. This is a critical correctness bug that must be fixed before any performance optimizations. Performance-wise, 930ms vs DuckDB's 77ms (12x slower) and our previous 386ms baseline suggests there may also be performance issues, but correctness takes absolute priority.

critical_fixes.issue: Incorrect aggregation results due to missing merge of parallel partial aggregations
critical_fixes.fix: Add explicit aggregation merge step before Top-K selection
critical_fixes.description: After line 282 (after all threads complete lineitem scan), add a merge phase that combines all agg_maps into a single global aggregation map. For each key that appears in multiple thread-local maps, sum the revenues. Then perform Top-K selection on the merged global map instead of the per-thread maps. Code change: (1) Create std::unordered_map<AggKey, double, AggKeyHash> global_agg; (2) Loop through all agg_maps[t], for each entry, do global_agg[key] += revenue; (3) Build Top-K heap from global_agg instead of from agg_maps. This ensures each order aggregates ALL its lineitems across all threads before ranking.

performance_optimizations.description: After fixing correctness, optimize the join order. Currently: (1) filter customer → build hash (300K entries, 20% selectivity), (2) scan orders and probe customer hash → build orders hash (~3M entries after filter), (3) scan lineitem and probe orders hash. The orders hash table is very large (3M entries). Consider inverting: filter orders first by date (o_orderdate < 1995-03-15, ~50% selectivity = 7.5M → fewer after customer join), then probe with lineitem. Better: filter lineitem by shipdate first (l_shipdate > 1995-03-15, ~50% selectivity = 30M rows), build hash on filtered lineitem orderkeys (distinct orderkeys ~10M → ~5M after filter), scan orders+customer join result (~1.5M rows after both filters), probe lineitem hash. Expected improvement: Reduces hash table size from 3M to ~1.5M, improves cache hit rate by ~2x in probe phase.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-20% reduction in execution time by reducing hash table size and improving cache locality

performance_optimizations.description: Storage design specifies zone maps on l_shipdate and o_orderdate with 100K row blocks. Currently, the code does full scans of orders and lineitem tables, checking predicates row-by-row. Implement zone map skipping: (1) Read zone map metadata files (e.g., lineitem.l_shipdate.zonemap containing min/max per 100K block), (2) For lineitem scan (line 259-274), skip blocks where max(l_shipdate) <= 1995-03-15 (predicate is l_shipdate > 1995-03-15), (3) For orders scan (line 213-218), skip blocks where min(o_orderdate) >= 1995-03-15. Given ~50% selectivity on both predicates and data sorted by these columns, expect to skip ~50% of blocks = ~50% I/O reduction. On HDD, this is critical for reducing seeks.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 30-40% speedup from reduced I/O (50% fewer blocks read). On HDD with 60M lineitem rows, reading 30M fewer rows saves significant disk seek time.

performance_optimizations.description: Hardware supports AVX512 (64 cores with avx512f flag detected). Apply SIMD to: (1) Date filtering: vectorize the comparisons in lineitem scan (l_shipdate > date_threshold) and orders scan (o_orderdate < date_threshold) using AVX2 to process 8 int32 dates per instruction, (2) Hash table probing: batch hash lookups by computing 8 hashes in parallel using SIMD hash functions (see knowledge/parallelism/simd.md), then perform 8 lookups, (3) Revenue calculation: vectorize the expression (price * (100 - disc) / 100 / 100) to compute 4 doubles per iteration using AVX2. Current code processes one row at a time. Expected: 3-4x speedup in filter/hash/arithmetic hot loops.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 20-30% overall speedup by accelerating CPU-bound portions (filtering ~15%, hash probing ~35%, arithmetic ~10% of runtime). May achieve 4x in individual loops but overall speedup is gated by hash table random access patterns.

performance_optimizations.technique: Optimize hash table performance with better hash function and table sizing
performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses std::unordered_map with a simple XOR-based hash (lines 117-121). std::unordered_map has poor cache behavior and high per-operation overhead. Improvements: (1) Pre-size all hash tables to avoid rehashing: qualified_customers.reserve(300000), qualified_orders.reserve(3000000), agg_maps[t].reserve(50000), (2) Replace XOR hash with MurmurHash or xxHash for better distribution, (3) Consider switching to robin_hood::unordered_map or absl::flat_hash_map (2-5x faster than std::unordered_map according to knowledge/data-structures/compact-hash-tables.md). Current: std::unordered_map has ~50-70% load factor and frequent cache misses. Expected: reserve() eliminates rehashing overhead (~5-10% speedup), better hash table implementation gives 2-3x speedup in join/aggregation.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-25% speedup from eliminating rehashing, additional 2x potential from switching to high-performance hash table (but requires external library)

performance_optimizations.technique: Optimize aggregation key structure and comparison
performance_optimizations.description: The aggregation key AggKey (lines 104-122) uses three int32 fields. The hash function (lines 116-121) is suboptimal: XOR-based with bit shifts, which has poor avalanche properties. The equality operator performs three comparisons. Improvements: (1) Pack the key into a single uint64_t or use a struct with padding eliminated (currently 12 bytes → align to 16 or pack to 12), (2) Use a single comparison for equality if packed, (3) Implement a faster hash function (multiplicative hash or use std::hash on uint64_t). Additionally, after merging thread-local maps, consider using a vector of pairs instead of unordered_map for the final global aggregation if cardinality is reasonable (<1M groups), as linear scan for Top-K may be faster than hash table iteration.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% speedup by reducing hash computation and equality check overhead in hot aggregation loop

summary: Q3 iteration 0 has a CRITICAL correctness bug: parallel aggregation does not merge partial sums from different threads before Top-K selection, resulting in revenues ~50% of correct values. This must be fixed immediately (priority 0 critical fix). After fixing, performance is 930ms vs DuckDB 77ms (12x slower), with primary bottlenecks: (1) Large hash tables (3M entries) causing poor cache behavior, (2) No zone map utilization despite sorted storage and available indexes, (3) Lack of SIMD vectorization for filtering/hashing on 64-core AVX512 system, (4) Suboptimal hash table implementation (std::unordered_map, no pre-sizing). Recommended optimization sequence: fix correctness first, then add zone map skipping (30-40% gain), optimize join order (10-20% gain), add SIMD (20-30% gain), improve hash tables (15-25% gain).
